content_html:
INF10025Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 8 – Part 2
Modelling
M:M relationships
SQL: Outer Joins, SubQueries,
Unions, Views

Conversion to Relational Schema
The ERD translates to the following relational schema:
PATRON (PatronNo, Name, Gender)
PK: PatronNo
TOY( ToyNo, Description, YearAquired)
PK: ToyNo
LOAN( PatronNo, ToyNo, DateBorrowed, DateReturned)
PK: PatronNo, ToyNo
FK1: PatronNo references PATRON
FK2: ToyNo references TOY
Is this correct???       Find out by testing with some sample data…

Testing the ERD
Generate some sample data for the Strong Entities
PatronNo 53	Sofia Vergara	Female
PatronNo 71	Alex Dyson	Male
ToyNo 27	Rocking Horse	2013
ToyNo 31	Spinning Top	2014
Generate some sample data for the Weak Entity
PatronNo	ToyNo	DateBorrow	DateReturn
53		27	1 June			8 June
53		31	1 June			8 June
53		27	19 July			30 July

Does the ERD match business rules
Can you see a problem with the 3rd Loan transaction?
PatronNo	ToyNo	DateBorrow	DateReturn
53		27	1 June			8 June
53		31	1 June			8 June
53		27	19 July			30 July
The PK of the 3rd row is a duplicate!    This will cause an error.This schema will not allow a Patron to Borrow any toy more than once.
It seems that thecurrent identifier of LOAN is incorrect / inadequate.

Testing Identifiers
A patron must be able to borrow the same toy on multiple dates.
So underline the DateBorrowed attribute.
The identifier is now PatronNo + ToyNo + DateBorrowed
Is this ERD correct? Test it with sample data …

Testing Identifiers
The relational schema for Loan is now:
LOAN( PatronNo, ToyNo, DateBorrowed, DateReturned)
PK: PatronNo, ToyNo, DateBorrowed
FK1: PatronNo references PATRON
FK2: ToyNo references TOY
Test the following Loan data rows. Will they be valid?
PatronNo	ToyNo	DateBorrow	DateReturn
53		27	1 June		8 June
53		31	1 June		8 June
53		27	19 July		30 July
53		27	2 August
71		27	11 June		18 June
71		31	11 June		18 June
71		28	31 July		30 August
53		31	9 October

Keep testing…
Would the ERD still be correct if we included DateReturned as part of the Loan Identifier?     Three possible answers exist
DateReturned should be part of Loan identifier
DateReturned must not be part of Loan identifier
It makes no difference to the outcome / usage

Testing Identifiers again
The relational schema for Loan is now:
LOAN( PatronNo, ToyNo, DateBorrowed, DateReturned)
PK: PatronNo, ToyNo, DateBorrowed, DateReturned
FK1: PatronNo references PATRON
FK2: ToyNo references TOY
Test the following Loan data rows. Will they be valid?
PatronNo	ToyNo	DateBorrow	DateReturn
53		27	1 June			8 June
53		27	19 July			30 July
53		27	2 August
A PK must not contain Nulls
Row 3 fails this test.

SQL: Multiple Topics
The following slides describe a number of SQL statements and clauses the we will consider to be 'advanced'.
Students hoping to secure only a Pass in this unit will not have to attempt these questions in PASS submission requirements or in Test number 2
Students seeking a higher grade should try some or all of the following:
Date functions
Outer Joins
Sub Queries
Unions
Constraints

SQL: Dates using Oracle
Some simple examples of using the Date Datatype within Oracle
With the TO_DATE functionYou may specify the format of the incoming date
CREATE TABLE STUDENT (
stuID   	        NUMBER  Primary Key
, birthdate      DATE
);
INSERT using DD-MON-YYYY date format
INSERT INTO STUDENT (stuid, birthdate)
VALUES (100,  '21-MAY-1996' );
INSERT using OTHER date format
INSERT INTO STUDENT (stuid, birthdate)
VALUES (100, TO_DATE( '21/05/1996', 'dd/mm/yyyy') );
Oracle default date format

SQL: Dates using Oracle
Some simple query examples of using the Date Datatype within Oracle
SELECT using DD-MON-YYYY date format
SELECT * FROM STUDENT WHERE birthdate = '21-MAY-1996';
SELECT using OTHER date format
SELECT * FROM STUDENT
WHERE birthdate >= (TO_DATE( '01/01/1996', 'dd/mm/yyyy')
AND      birthdate <= (TO_DATE( '31/12/2016', 'dd/mm/yyyy')
);

SQL: SubQueries
We have already examined the AVG aggregate function.
E.g. SELECT AVG(runtime) From Movie;         --calculates the average movie length
Suppose we need to find all movies longer than the average.
You might try this:
SELECT * FROM MOVIE
WHERE    runtime >  AVG(runtime);
This is incorrect.
Aggregation functions are NOT permitted in the WHERE clause.
Why?
An aggregation function must be evaluated in the context of its own query.
Solution: use a subquery.
🗶

SQL:Subqueries Example 1
A SUBQUERY is a complete SQL statement that is placed WITHIN an existing SQL statement.
Subqueries are often used when a result cannot be calculated by a single SQL statement. (The outcome requires two or more SQL statements)
Subqueries ARE permitted within the WHERE clause.
The subquery is evaluated first (takes precedence) because it is in brackets.
The subquery is executed ONCE. It is not executed separately for every row.
SELECT  * FROM    MOVIE
WHERE   runtime  >   (    	SELECT AVG(runtime)			     						FROM     MOVIE              );

SQL: Sub Queries dissected
SELECT *
FROM     movie
WHERE  runtime >  ( SELECT    AVG(runtime) FROM       movie )   ;
The subquery is evaluated first because it is in brackets.
See BODMAS    http://www.mathsisfun.com/operation-order-bodmas.html
This subquery returns a single value
For each row in the movie table, the runtime value is compared to the value returned by the subquery.

SQL:Subqueries  Example 2
Find the movie with the longest duration.
First, determine the query to find the longest duration
SELECT MAX(runtime)
FROM MOVIE;
Now write the other code that interacts with the subquery
SELECT *
FROM MOVIE
WHERE runtime = ( result of subquery );
Put it all together in a single statement
SELECT *
FROM MOVIE
WHERE runtime = (  SELECT   MAX(runtime)
FROM     MOVIE
);

SQL:Subqueries  Example 3
“Find all movies with Canadian Actors”
Sub Query with Where In
First, determine actor numbers of actors born in Canada
SELECT actorno FROM actor WHERE bornin = 'Canada';
Now write the other code that interacts with the subquery
SELECT m.title
FROM movie m
INNER JOIN casting c
ON m.movieno = c.movieno
WHERE actorno IN  ( result of subquery );
Put it all together in a single statement
SELECT m.title
FROM movie m	INNER JOIN casting c
ON m.movieno = c.movieno
WHERE actorno IN (SELECT actorno FROM actor WHERE bornin = 'Canada');

SQL:Subqueries  Example 4
“Which rating codes have an average runtime greater than the average runtime for all movies?”
Sub Query in the Having clause
First, determine the query to find average runtime
SELECT AVG(runtime) FROM movie;
Now write the other code that interacts with the subquery
SELECT rating_code, AVG(runtime)
FROM movie
GROUP BY rating_code
HAVING AVG(runtime) >  ( result of subquery );
Put it all together in a single statement
SELECT rating_code, AVG(runtime)
FROM movie
GROUP BY rating_code
HAVING AVG(runtime) >  (SELECT AVG(runtime) FROM movie;
https://www.essentialsql.com/get-ready-to-learn-sql-server-19-introduction-to-sub-queries/

Types of Joins
Consider the data in table 1 and table 2.
Assume there is a PK:FK relationship between the tables.
A Select statement with an inner join gets data where PK=FK

INNER JOIN
An INNER JOIN uses
Values that have a match in both tables

SQL: Inner Joins
Business Requirement:
"Provide a list showing all actors. List the movies that each appears in"
SELECT       	a.fullname, c.movieno
FROM           	actor a
INNER JOIN 	casting c
ON                	c.actorno  = a.actorno
ORDER BY   	a.fullname;

What has happened to Brad Pitt?
He is NOT in the result set!
Requirement is not met.

LEFT OUTER JOIN
Imagine that we want all rows from Table 1
and all matching rows from Table 2.
This is known as a LEFT OUTER JOIN (because table 1 is on the left!)

SQL: Outer Joins
Business Requirement:
"Provide a list showing all actors. List the movies that each appears in"
SELECT       	        a.fullname, c.movieno
FROM           	        actor a
LEFT OUTER JOIN   casting c
ON                	        c.actorno  = a.actorno
ORDER BY   	        a.fullname;

SQL: Inner & Outer Joins
The Inner Join gathers datafrom casting and movie.The Outer Join then adds the fullname of ALL actors
SELECT       	          a.fullname, c.movieno, m.title
FROM           	          casting c
INNER JOIN               movie m
ON                              c.movieno = m.movieno
RIGHT OUTER JOIN  actor a
ON                	          c.actorno  = a.actorno
ORDER BY   	          a.actorno;
